nativeRange.habitat: Slow-moving, heavily vegetated coastal streams and tributaries in the Rio de Janeiro basin; typically found in soft, slightly acidic, tea-stained blackwater over leaf-litter and sandy substrates

decorPreferences: dense planting (Vallisneria, Java moss, Amazon sword)
driftwood and dried leaf litter for tannins and shelter
dark substrate to bring out red coloration
open midwater swimming area in front of planting

diet.notes: Accepts virtually all small aquarium foods: high-quality micro-pellets, crushed flake, and frozen or live foods such as brine shrimp nauplii, micro-worms, daphnia, and bloodworm. Wild fish feed on small invertebrates, zooplankton, algae, and plant matter. A varied diet including some live or frozen food enhances color and conditioning for breeding.

goodWith: other small peaceful tetras (neon tetra, ember tetra)
freshwater shrimp (cherry shrimp and larger neocaridina)
avoidWith: very small nano shrimp (fry may be eaten)

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species photo showing characteristic red flanks and two black shoulder bars; check current availability at fishbase.se summary page.
Wikimedia Commons image — verify license and resolution before use.

summary: A small, brilliantly colored Brazilian endemic prized for its intense red-orange flanks and two distinctive black shoulder bars. Cool-tolerant, peaceful, and easy to keep; Critically Endangered in the wild due to urbanization of its native Rio de Janeiro streams.
careNotes: The Flame Tetra is one of the hardiest and most beginner-friendly tetras in the hobby, yet it remains Critically Endangered in its native Rio de Janeiro coastal drainages due to near-total habitat loss from urban expansion. In the aquarium it is robustly adaptable — it tolerates a wider pH (5.5–7.5) and temperature range (20–26°C) than many soft-water tetras, making it suitable for unheated tanks in temperate rooms. A group of at least 8 (ideally 10–12) is recommended: larger shoals reduce skittishness, intensify the collective red display, and allow natural mid-water schooling behaviour. Males are noticeably slimmer and more vividly coloured than females; ripe females become plump with eggs. The two distinctive black humeral (shoulder) bars behind the operculum immediately separate H. flammeus from superficially similar species: the Serpae Tetra (H. eques) has a single comma-shaped spot, while the Yellow Phantom Tetra has an elongated mark and lacks the red body colouration. Soft, slightly acidic water with tannin staining (from driftwood or dried Indian almond leaves) replicates the natural blackwater biotope and promotes the deepest colouration. Dark substrate amplifies the fish's red hues. Avoid bright overhead lighting without floating cover. Water quality should be maintained with weekly 25–30% water changes; the species is intolerant of high nitrates. Most aquarium stock is captive-bred and more adaptable than wild fish, but wild-caught individuals benefit from a careful pH and temperature acclimation period.
breedingNotes: Egg scatterers that spawn freely among fine-leaved plants or spawning mops. Condition a pair or small group on live and frozen foods for 1–2 weeks, then move to a dedicated 20–40 L breeding tank with soft acidic water (pH 6.0–6.8, gH 1–5, temperature 24–26°C) and clumps of Java moss or a spawning mop. Spawning typically occurs in the morning in response to gentle filtration flow and dimmed light. The pair engage in side-by-side vibrating spawning runs, scattering up to 200 adhesive eggs across plant leaves and substrate. Parents will consume eggs and fry; remove adults immediately after spawning is observed. Eggs hatch in 24–48 hours; fry become free-swimming in 3–5 days and can be fed infusoria or commercial fry food initially, graduating to baby brine shrimp nauplii within a week. Breeding difficulty is rated easy for experienced aquarists willing to set up a separate conditioning and spawning tank.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Hyphessobrycon flammeus
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (3–4 cm TL), temperature range (20–26°C), pH (5.5–7.5), hardness (1–15 dH), native range (Rio de Janeiro coastal drainages, Brazil), conservation status (Critically Endangered, IUCN), biotope, diet, breeding behaviour, and conspecific identification notes distinguishing H. flammeus from H. eques and other red tetras.

additional.name: FishBase — Hyphessobrycon flammeus
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (max 4.5 cm TL), temperature range, native range (coastal Rio de Janeiro basin), taxonomy (Characidae, Characiformes), IUCN Critically Endangered status confirmed. Confirmed two black humeral bars as diagnostic character.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Flame Tetra Care Guide
additional.notes: Trade source confirming beginner care level, tank size recommendation (10 gal / ~40 L minimum for a small group, 20 gal / ~75 L recommended), diet acceptance of flake and frozen foods, peaceful temperament, and group size advice of 6+. Conservative 60 L minimum applied per schema playbook (fits 8-fish minimum group).

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Hyphessobrycon flammeus species profile
additional.notes: Supplementary source confirming cool-tolerance, identification features (two black shoulder bars vs Serpae single spot), spawning behaviour (egg scatterer, fine-leaved plants), and UK-relevant note that fish can be kept in unheated tanks in average-temperature homes.

additional.name: IUCN Red List — Hyphessobrycon flammeus
additional.notes: Confirmed Critically Endangered (CR) status. Primary threat is severe habitat degradation and loss in the Guanabara Bay drainage system (Rio de Janeiro metropolitan area) due to urbanisation, pollution, and deforestation. Wild populations now restricted to heavily fragmented habitat; hobbyist stock is almost entirely captive-bred.

fish.breedingNotes: Egg scatterer; spawns freely among fine-leaved plants or spawning mops in soft, acidic water (pH 6.0–6.8). Up to ~200 eggs per spawn. Remove adults post-spawn to prevent egg predation. Fry free-swimming in 3–5 days; feed infusoria then baby brine shrimp nauplii.
